summary: Stellar China>Rider close, jam‑heavy second set
blurb: The first set builds to a surprise China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider finale and the second set rolls from Hey Pocky Way through Ship Of Fools into tight Playing In The Band → Eyes Of The World. Brent’s Hammond shines and Phil’s bass roars, making it a must‑listen for ’89 fans.
review: On May 6, 1989 at Frost Amphitheatre the Dead delivered one of their most memorable spring outings. A surprise China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider closes an already solid first set, and the second set erupts with Hey Pocky Way, a fiery Samson & Delilah, and a seamless Ship Of Fools → Playing In The Band → Eyes Of The World that showcases the band’s improvisational fire. Jerry Garcia throws down scorching solos on Ship Of Fools and Pocky Way, even if his lyrics stumble on Jack Straw and his vocal mix can get muddy. Brent Mydland’s Hammond organ is vibrant throughout, especially on Pocky Way, while Phil Lesh’s low‑end thunders with a “monster” subsonic presence that some listeners had to turn down. The rhythm section stays tight, driving the jams forward without complaint. Highlights also include Let The Good Times Roll, The Race Is On, and a soulful Black Muddy River encore. With crystal‑clear soundboard sources capturing every nuance, this show stands out as a high‑energy snapshot of the band in peak ’89 form.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Three recordings are explicitly positive, praising the China>Rider close, Pocky Way, Ship Of Fools and Brent's keyboards; two mixed reviews still note strong performances with only minor drawbacks. No negative consensus appears, so a strong positive adjustment of +1.4 is applied.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear soundboard mix that captures the standout China>Rider, Pocky Way and Ship Of Fools moments with rich highs and solid lows.

key_highlights: Surprise China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider close to first set
Jam‑heavy second set chain: Hey Pocky Way → Samson & Delilah → Ship Of Fools → Playing In The Band → Eyes Of The World
Brent Mydland’s vibrant Hammond work, especially on Pocky Way
Phil Lesh’s booming low end noted as a “monster in the subsonics”

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers scorching solos on Ship Of Fools and Pocky Way; occasional lyric slip on Jack Straw and vocals sound muddy at times, but overall energetic playing.
band_performance.Phil: Bass is clear, driving and exceptionally low‑end heavy; praised for a powerful subsonic presence that anchors the jams.
band_performance.Brent: Vibrant Hammond organ work shines throughout, with fiery textures on Pocky Way and solid keyboard support across the set.
band_performance.Mickey: Drums stay tight and energetic; no specific criticisms noted.
band_performance.Billy: Provides a steady, propulsive pulse alongside Mickey; rhythm section praised for its cohesion.
